The system should handle Facebook-scale traffic (~2B DAU) and support features like reactions, live notifications, and seamless user transitions between different content.

Were You Asked This?
Design Facebook Live Comments

Design a real-time commenting system that allows users to view and post comments instantly while watching live videos or browsing posts across Meta platforms. The system should handle Facebook-scale traffic (~2B DAU) and support features like reactions, live notifications, and seamless user transitions between different content.

Asked at:

Meta

N

Nubank

Oracle

Google

+1 More
Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

Facebook Live Comments is a real-time commenting experience embedded in live video on Facebook and Instagram, where millions of viewers post, react, and see new messages stream in sub-second.

Interviewers ask this to test whether you can design low-latency fanout at massive scale, reason about hot-spot contention on a single stream, and handle unreliable mobile clients that disconnect, background, and switch streams rapidly. Strong answers connect product needs (smooth scrolling, no gaps/dupes, graceful degradation) to the right architectural patterns (pub/sub, cursors, replay buffers, backpressure) and client-side strategies.

Hello Interview Problem Breakdown
FB Live Comments

System design answer key for designing a real-time commenting system like Facebook Live, built by FAANG managers and staff engineers.

View Answer Key
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to post comments and reactions to an ongoing live video.

Users should be able to see new comments appear in real time without manual refresh.

Users should be able to view recent comments from before they joined the stream to get context.

Users should be able to switch between live videos and immediately see only the relevant comments without gaps or duplicates.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How do you route live updates to the correct realtime server as users join, leave, or rapidly switch streams?

How will you scale fanout for a hot stream with millions of concurrent viewers while keeping end-to-end latency under ~500 ms?

What delivery guarantees and catch-up strategy ensure viewers don’t miss comments during disconnects or app backgrounding?

What are the major client-side challenges on mobile (battery, backgrounding, flaky networks) and how do you design the protocol and UI to handle them?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Live comments are fundamentally a push problem: you need persistent connections, pub/sub topics keyed by stream, and a dispatch path that delivers new messages to millions of viewers within hundreds of milliseconds.

Read More
Scaling Reads

Each comment is read orders of magnitude more times than it’s written, especially for celebrity streams. Designing caches, partitioned topics, and efficient fanout trees lets you scale reads cheaply without overloading the write path.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

A single hot liveVideoId becomes a hot key. You must shard fanout, apply backpressure, and enforce fairness so one stream doesn’t starve others or collapse the realtime tier.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka provides a durable, partitioned log per liveVideoId for write decoupling and replay. It enables catch-up via offsets, supports high throughput fanout, and smooths spikes from hot streams.

Read More
Redis

Redis excels for low-latency presence and subscription state, recent-comment buffers with TTL for fast reconnect, lightweight rate limiting counters, and quick pub/sub where durability isn’t required.

Read More
ZooKeeper

ZooKeeper can coordinate shard membership, maintain consistent hashing rings, and support leader election or rebalancing so liveVideoId-to-edge mappings stay correct during failures and scaling events.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

WhatsApp

Group chats mirror per-stream channels: presence, ordering, reconnect semantics, and at-least-once delivery with client-side dedup are shared concerns.

Read
Practice
YouTube

YouTube Live chat faces identical hot-stream fanout, moderation, sampling under load, and replay for late joiners—nearly the same architecture with different product polish.

Read
Practice
FB News Feed

While not strictly realtime, News Feed highlights scaling reads, fanout vs. fan-in trade-offs, and connection to ranking/caching strategies relevant to showing top or most recent comments.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Broadcasting all comments to all realtime servers and filtering locally

This forces every server to process every message, wasting CPU and network, and it collapses under celebrity load. It breaks horizontal scaling and increases tail latency.

No reconnect/catch-up plan (no cursors, no replay buffer, no client dedup)

Mobile users will see gaps or duplicates whenever networks flap or the app backgrounds. Without sequence IDs and bounded replay, you cannot provide a coherent timeline.

Ignoring viewer co-location and using round-robin load balancing

Scattering subscribers for a single stream across many edges forces expensive cross-server fanout and increases latency and cost. Hot keys become unmanageable and failure domains expand.

SharedTurquoiseChameleon913
Top 10%
• 7个月前

I was asked this question for senior software engineer role for meta. I followed the framework, captured functional requirement, non functional requirement, entities and APIs. I gave a high level solution using functional requirement which just have 1 service and used post and get call(get calls were polled by client) and with dive deep reached to the solution with 2 services(I did some math to justify this, also justified this by saying that one service is for handling APIs and another one was managing SSEs) one for accepting post request for comment and other for SSE and I used the pub/sub between them. I also allocated videos to servers so that not all the server subscribe to all the topic and used zookeeper for co-ordination for servers. I used consistent hashing to assign a video to server. Also discussed failure scenario for adding/removing a server.

One part which i thought i did not solve in optimised way is for handling the spike. I calculated that let say if all user commented at the same time the bandwidth might go to 100 Mbps which could be very high so i introduced a queue to handle spike, this might add some delay but can protect the system and client. I guess there can be better aproaches for this.

I thought i did good but i got no hire from the interviewer, here are the major reasons which were provided:

The initial design was completely wrong and unoptimised which is a major red flag.(I guess this was for design for capturing functional requirement or maybe i used just 1 service in start, BTW i mentioned that i am using this to capture just the functional requirement and it may not be optimised, i will fix the bottleneck in deep dive).
No deep dive on client side(I did a dive deep on both the services, i thought that should be good enough for senior software interview role).
No discussion on failure scenarios(I am not sure why was this the case, i clearly discussed handling server going down for both the services. Maybe he was expecting client side handling as well).
The spike scenario was not handled properly from client perspective, candidate tried to solve it from backend system itself(As mentioned above).

After feedback i was suprised but realised the interviewer wanted me to focus more on the client side optimisation or wanted me to deep dive more on client side.

So just a suggestion to the people who might get this question for interview, either try to do the deep dive on client side as well or during dive deep decide with interviewer what component would he like dive deep on so that there might be no suprises later.

6

this is what makes interviewing a matter of luck, two candidates - same soltuion, one gets hired and the other is rejected, because the interviewer was different or had something else in mind.

Comments

Design a real-time commenting system that allows users to view and post comments instantly while watching live videos or browsing posts across Meta platforms. The system should handle Facebook-scale traffic (~2B DAU) and support features like reactions, live notifications, and seamless user transitions between different content.

Asked at:

Meta

N

Nubank

Oracle

Google

+1 More
Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

Facebook Live Comments is a real-time commenting experience embedded in live video on Facebook and Instagram, where millions of viewers post, react, and see new messages stream in sub-second.

Interviewers ask this to test whether you can design low-latency fanout at massive scale, reason about hot-spot contention on a single stream, and handle unreliable mobile clients that disconnect, background, and switch streams rapidly. Strong answers connect product needs (smooth scrolling, no gaps/dupes, graceful degradation) to the right architectural patterns (pub/sub, cursors, replay buffers, backpressure) and client-side strategies.

Hello Interview Problem Breakdown
FB Live Comments

System design answer key for designing a real-time commenting system like Facebook Live, built by FAANG managers and staff engineers.

View Answer Key
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to post comments and reactions to an ongoing live video.

Users should be able to see new comments appear in real time without manual refresh.

Users should be able to view recent comments from before they joined the stream to get context.

Users should be able to switch between live videos and immediately see only the relevant comments without gaps or duplicates.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How do you route live updates to the correct realtime server as users join, leave, or rapidly switch streams?

How will you scale fanout for a hot stream with millions of concurrent viewers while keeping end-to-end latency under ~500 ms?

What delivery guarantees and catch-up strategy ensure viewers don’t miss comments during disconnects or app backgrounding?

What are the major client-side challenges on mobile (battery, backgrounding, flaky networks) and how do you design the protocol and UI to handle them?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Live comments are fundamentally a push problem: you need persistent connections, pub/sub topics keyed by stream, and a dispatch path that delivers new messages to millions of viewers within hundreds of milliseconds.

Read More
Scaling Reads

Each comment is read orders of magnitude more times than it’s written, especially for celebrity streams. Designing caches, partitioned topics, and efficient fanout trees lets you scale reads cheaply without overloading the write path.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

A single hot liveVideoId becomes a hot key. You must shard fanout, apply backpressure, and enforce fairness so one stream doesn’t starve others or collapse the realtime tier.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka provides a durable, partitioned log per liveVideoId for write decoupling and replay. It enables catch-up via offsets, supports high throughput fanout, and smooths spikes from hot streams.

Read More
Redis

Redis excels for low-latency presence and subscription state, recent-comment buffers with TTL for fast reconnect, lightweight rate limiting counters, and quick pub/sub where durability isn’t required.

Read More
ZooKeeper

ZooKeeper can coordinate shard membership, maintain consistent hashing rings, and support leader election or rebalancing so liveVideoId-to-edge mappings stay correct during failures and scaling events.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

WhatsApp

Group chats mirror per-stream channels: presence, ordering, reconnect semantics, and at-least-once delivery with client-side dedup are shared concerns.

Read
Practice
YouTube

YouTube Live chat faces identical hot-stream fanout, moderation, sampling under load, and replay for late joiners—nearly the same architecture with different product polish.

Read
Practice
FB News Feed

While not strictly realtime, News Feed highlights scaling reads, fanout vs. fan-in trade-offs, and connection to ranking/caching strategies relevant to showing top or most recent comments.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Broadcasting all comments to all realtime servers and filtering locally

This forces every server to process every message, wasting CPU and network, and it collapses under celebrity load. It breaks horizontal scaling and increases tail latency.

No reconnect/catch-up plan (no cursors, no replay buffer, no client dedup)

Mobile users will see gaps or duplicates whenever networks flap or the app backgrounds. Without sequence IDs and bounded replay, you cannot provide a coherent timeline.

Ignoring viewer co-location and using round-robin load balancing

Scattering subscribers for a single stream across many edges forces expensive cross-server fanout and increases latency and cost. Hot keys become unmanageable and failure domains expand.

I was asked this question for senior software engineer role for meta. I followed the framework, captured functional requirement, non functional requirement, entities and APIs. I gave a high level solution using functional requirement which just have 1 service and used post and get call(get calls were polled by client) and with dive deep reached to the solution with 2 services(I did some math to justify this, also justified this by saying that one service is for handling APIs and another one was managing SSEs) one for accepting post request for comment and other for SSE and I used the pub/sub between them. I also allocated videos to servers so that not all the server subscribe to all the topic and used zookeeper for co-ordination for servers. I used consistent hashing to assign a video to server. Also discussed failure scenario for adding/removing a server.

One part which i thought i did not solve in optimised way is for handling the spike. I calculated that let say if all user commented at the same time the bandwidth might go to 100 Mbps which could be very high so i introduced a queue to handle spike, this might add some delay but can protect the system and client. I guess there can be better aproaches for this.

I thought i did good but i got no hire from the interviewer, here are the major reasons which were provided:

The initial design was completely wrong and unoptimised which is a major red flag.(I guess this was for design for capturing functional requirement or maybe i used just 1 service in start, BTW i mentioned that i am using this to capture just the functional requirement and it may not be optimised, i will fix the bottleneck in deep dive).
No deep dive on client side(I did a dive deep on both the services, i thought that should be good enough for senior software interview role).
No discussion on failure scenarios(I am not sure why was this the case, i clearly discussed handling server going down for both the services. Maybe he was expecting client side handling as well).
The spike scenario was not handled properly from client perspective, candidate tried to solve it from backend system itself(As mentioned above).

After feedback i was suprised but realised the interviewer wanted me to focus more on the client side optimisation or wanted me to deep dive more on client side.

So just a suggestion to the people who might get this question for interview, either try to do the deep dive on client side as well or during dive deep decide with interviewer what component would he like dive deep on so that there might be no suprises later.

6

meta.title: Design Facebook Live Comments
meta.description: Design a real-time commenting system that allows users to view and post comments instantly while watching live videos or browsing posts across Meta platforms. The system should handle Facebook-scale traffic (~2B DAU) and support features like reactions, live notifications, and seamless user transitions between different content.
